title: Evaluation, the Balanced Scorecard & Governance
simple_html:
Once a strategy is chosen and put into action, the work is still not finished. Strategy evaluation is the final stage of the strategic-management process: checking whether the strategy is still working, catching problems early, and making corrections. Because the business world changes constantly, even a great strategy can become outdated - evaluation is the early-warning system.

The three core activities

  • Review the bases of strategy: are the assumptions about customers, competitors, and the environment still true?
  • Measure performance: compare actual results against expected results and against the goals you set.
  • Take corrective action: adjust the strategy, structure, or execution when there is a gap.

The Balanced Scorecard

Relying only on financial numbers is like driving while looking in the rear-view mirror - they tell you where you have been, not where you are going. The Balanced Scorecard fixes this by measuring four perspectives together: Financial, Customer, Internal Business Processes, and Learning & Growth. This gives a fuller, more forward-looking picture of health.

Governance

Corporate governance is the system of oversight - chiefly the board of directors - that ensures managers act in the owners' (and stakeholders') interests. It addresses the "agency problem": the risk that managers pursue their own goals instead of the company's.

Key takeaways

  • Evaluation closes the loop: formulate, implement, then check and correct.
  • Use both financial and non-financial measures (the Balanced Scorecard).
  • Evaluate continuously, not once a year - the environment moves fast.
  • Good governance aligns managers with owners and protects stakeholders.
intermediate_html:
Strategy evaluation is the capstone of the strategic-management process. Strategies are formulated and implemented under assumptions that inevitably change, so firms need a disciplined mechanism to confirm that a strategy is still appropriate and effective - and to correct course when it is not. Evaluation also connects to governance, because the board of directors is ultimately responsible for ensuring that management's strategy serves the owners and stakeholders.

The three strategy-evaluation activities

Rumelt's and David's frameworks converge on three activities:

  1. Examining the underlying bases of strategy: revisiting the EFE/IFE/CPM and asking whether the external opportunities/threats and internal strengths/weaknesses that justified the strategy still hold.
  2. Measuring organizational performance: comparing actual to expected results, evaluating progress toward objectives, and analyzing variances.
  3. Taking corrective actions: making changes to reposition the firm - altering structure, replacing personnel, revising objectives, raising capital, or even changing the strategy itself.

Rumelt's tests of strategy

Richard Rumelt (1980) proposed four criteria for evaluating a strategy: Consistency (no mutually inconsistent goals), Consonance (an adaptive response to the external environment), Feasibility (within resource limits), and Advantage (creates or maintains competitive advantage). These are a useful qualitative checklist before relying on quantitative measures.

The Balanced Scorecard

Developed by Kaplan and Norton (1992), the Balanced Scorecard counters the over-reliance on financial metrics, which are lagging indicators. It evaluates performance across four balanced perspectives, each with objectives, measures, targets, and initiatives:

PerspectiveQuestion it answersExample measure
FinancialHow do we look to shareholders?ROI, revenue growth
CustomerHow do customers see us?Satisfaction, retention
Internal ProcessesWhat must we excel at?Cycle time, defect rate
Learning & GrowthCan we keep improving?Training hours, employee retention

The power of the scorecard is its causal logic: investments in learning and growth improve internal processes, which improve customer outcomes, which ultimately drive financial results.

Corporate governance and agency theory

Governance is the system by which firms are directed and controlled. The central problem it addresses is the agency problem articulated by Jensen and Meckling (1976): because owners (principals) delegate to managers (agents), and interests can diverge, mechanisms are needed to align them. The board of directors is the primary internal mechanism - monitoring management, approving strategy, and protecting shareholder and stakeholder interests. Governance reforms (e.g., post-Sarbanes-Oxley independence and audit requirements) strengthen board oversight.

Worked mini-example

A software firm set an annual objective of 20% revenue growth but achieved 12%. Evaluation proceeds: (1) Review bases - a new entrant has intensified rivalry (an EFE change), so a key assumption has shifted. (2) Measure - customer churn rose from 5% to 9%, a Balanced-Scorecard customer-perspective red flag, while learning-and-growth metrics show under-investment in product engineers. (3) Corrective action - reallocate budget to retention and R&D, revise the objective to 15%, and report the variance to the board, which under its governance role probes whether management's incentives encouraged short-term sales over retention.

How to apply it

  1. Build a Balanced Scorecard with measures across all four perspectives - not just finance.
  2. Re-audit the strategic bases regularly using updated EFE/IFE.
  3. Run Rumelt's four tests as a qualitative screen.
  4. Define corrective-action triggers in advance.
  5. Ensure the board independently reviews strategy and aligns incentives.

Exam tip: memorize the three evaluation activities, the four scorecard perspectives, Rumelt's four tests, and the principal-agent definition of the agency problem.
full_html:
Strategy evaluation completes the strategic-management cycle, transforming it from a linear plan into a continuous, self-correcting loop. Because strategies are built on assumptions about a dynamic environment, and because implementation rarely unfolds exactly as designed, firms require a disciplined apparatus to verify that a strategy remains valid and effective. This lesson integrates three intertwined themes: the evaluation process itself, the Balanced Scorecard as its leading measurement architecture, and corporate governance as the oversight system that holds management accountable for strategic outcomes. It builds directly on formulation (Lesson 6) and implementation (Lesson 7) and closes the loop opened in Lesson 1.

The nature and necessity of evaluation

Evaluation is increasingly difficult because environments change faster, time horizons shorten, and interdependencies multiply. Yet it is indispensable: an unexamined strategy can drift into obsolescence precisely when it appears most successful. The classic structure comprises three activities (David, 2017): (1) reviewing the underlying bases of strategy; (2) measuring organizational performance; and (3) taking corrective actions. The first re-examines whether the external and internal factors that justified the strategy still obtain, typically by reconstructing revised EFE, IFE, and CPM matrices and comparing them to the originals. The second compares expected to actual results and assesses progress toward stated objectives. The third repositions the firm - revising objectives, altering structure, reallocating resources, replacing personnel, or changing the strategy outright.

Rumelt's criteria

Richard Rumelt's four tests provide a rigorous qualitative screen (Rumelt, 1980): Consistency - the strategy must not present mutually inconsistent goals and policies; Consonance - it must represent an adaptive response to the external environment and the critical changes occurring within it; Feasibility - it must neither overtax resources nor create unsolvable subproblems; and Advantage - it must provide for the creation or maintenance of competitive advantage. A strategy that fails any test is suspect regardless of favorable short-term financials. These criteria usefully complement quantitative measurement by exposing structural flaws that metrics alone may miss.

The Balanced Scorecard

The dominant critique of traditional evaluation is its over-reliance on financial measures, which are lagging indicators - they report the consequences of past decisions but say little about future capacity. Kaplan and Norton's Balanced Scorecard (Kaplan and Norton, 1992; 1996) resolves this by measuring performance across four mutually reinforcing perspectives, each populated with objectives, measures, targets, and initiatives:

PerspectiveStrategic questionRepresentative measures
FinancialHow do we appear to shareholders?ROI, ROCE, economic value added, revenue growth
CustomerHow do customers perceive us?Satisfaction, retention, market share, acquisition cost
Internal Business ProcessesAt what must we excel?Cycle time, quality/defect rate, productivity
Learning & GrowthCan we continue to improve and create value?Employee skills, information-system capability, retention

The framework's deeper contribution, developed in their later work on strategy maps (Kaplan and Norton, 2004), is an explicit causal hypothesis: investments in learning and growth drive better internal processes, which produce superior customer outcomes, which ultimately yield financial performance. The scorecard thus operationalizes strategy into a coherent, communicable set of leading and lagging indicators and serves as a strategic-management system, not merely a measurement dashboard. Critics note risks of metric overload, weak causal validation between perspectives, and gaming of measures - so the scorecard must be curated, not bloated.

Corporate governance and agency theory

Evaluation ultimately answers to ownership, which makes governance integral to this lesson. Corporate governance is the system of rules, practices, and processes by which a firm is directed and controlled, balancing the interests of shareholders, management, customers, suppliers, financiers, government, and the community. Its theoretical foundation is agency theory (Jensen and Meckling, 1976; Fama and Jensen, 1983): the separation of ownership (principals) from control (agents) creates the risk that self-interested managers pursue private benefits - empire-building, excessive perquisites, risk-aversion - at owners' expense. This generates agency costs: monitoring costs, bonding costs, and residual loss.

Mechanisms to mitigate the agency problem include the board of directors (the primary internal monitor responsible for hiring/firing the CEO, approving strategy, and overseeing risk), incentive alignment through equity-based compensation, the market for corporate control (takeover threat), auditing and disclosure, and concentrated ownership by institutional investors. Regulatory responses such as Sarbanes-Oxley (2002) strengthened board independence, audit-committee requirements, and executive accountability. A broader debate contrasts the shareholder primacy view (Friedman, 1970) with the stakeholder view (Freeman, 1984), which argues the board's duty extends to all parties with a stake in the firm - a tension directly relevant to how performance should be defined and evaluated.

Worked mini-example

A consumer-electronics firm reports record quarterly profit yet its board orders an evaluation. Bases review: a revised EFE reveals a disruptive low-cost entrant and a regulatory shift - consonance is weakening. Performance measurement via the scorecard: financial metrics are strong (lagging), but customer Net Promoter Score has fallen and learning-and-growth shows R&D headcount cuts - leading indicators are deteriorating. Rumelt's tests: the strategy now fails consonance and threatens future advantage. Corrective action: reinvest in R&D, reposition toward a defensible niche, and revise objectives. Governance: the board investigates whether an executive bonus plan tied solely to quarterly profit incentivized the harmful R&D cuts - an agency-cost diagnosis - and redesigns incentives to include scorecard-based, longer-horizon metrics.

Common mistakes and misconceptions

  • Evaluating only financially: ignoring leading indicators until the financials finally collapse.
  • Treating evaluation as annual: the environment changes continuously, so should review.
  • Metric overload: a scorecard with dozens of measures loses focus and invites gaming.
  • Confusing the scorecard with mere reporting: its purpose is to manage and communicate strategy, not just to tabulate it.
  • Board passivity: rubber-stamp boards fail their monitoring duty and amplify agency costs.
  • Ignoring incentive design: misaligned executive pay is a root cause of both strategy drift and governance failure.

In conclusion, strategy evaluation, the Balanced Scorecard, and governance form an integrated accountability system. Evaluation re-tests assumptions and performance; the scorecard supplies a balanced, forward-looking measurement architecture; and governance, grounded in agency theory, ensures that those who control the firm answer for results to those who own it and to the broader set of stakeholders. Together they convert strategic management from a one-time plan into a disciplined, self-correcting, and accountable cycle.
